@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/sensory processing / occupational therapy / interoception / thermoregulation / sensory modulation/temperature sensitivity / thermal regulation / hot-cold preferences / sensory over-responsivity / sensory under-responsivity / interoceptive awareness/9-materials-that-help-with-temperature-preferences#faq

mainEntity.name: Why does my child experiences temperature in ways that don't match anyone else in our family. some days they insist they're freezing when we're all comfortable. other days they're overheating while everyone else is fine. getting them to wear weather-appropriate clothing is an ongoing battle — they refuse jackets in winter because they 'feel fine' or strip off layers before we even leave the house in summer because 'it's too hot.' bath time is complicated. the water is either too hot or too cold, and the acceptable range is impossibly narrow. sometimes what was perfect yesterday is unbearable today. foods have to be the 'right' temperature or they won't eat. drinks must be ice cold or won't be touched. some foods can't touch their lips if they're too warm. touching cold surfaces — metal, tile, cold water — causes major reactions. they recoil from cold like it physically hurts. or the opposite: they seek out extreme cold or heat in ways that seem unsafe. they don't seem to notice when they're getting too hot until they're dysregulated, red-faced, and having a meltdown. they can't tell me when they're too cold until they're miserable. summer activities, winter activities, travel to different climates — everything is harder because of how they experience temperature. i've watched other children move through temperature changes seamlessly while mine struggles to cope with what seems like normal variation. i don't know if their body doesn't register temperature correctly, or registers it too intensely, or both at different times. what i know is that temperature affects their ability to participate in life, and i need strategies to help them?

acceptedAnswer.text: TEMPERATURE SENSORY PROCESSING DIFFERENCES / THERMAL SENSORY MODULATION DISORDER / INTEROCEPTIVE PROCESSING DIFFERENCES (TEMPERATURE) / THERMOREGULATORY DYSFUNCTION / SENSORY OVER-RESPONSIVITY TO TEMPERATURE / SENSORY UNDER-RESPONSIVITY TO TEMPERATURE! Temperature processing differences refer to atypical responses to thermal sensory input. These differences can manifest as: over-responsivity (hypersensitivity) where normal temperature variations feel extreme and uncomfortable, triggering avoidance or distress; under-responsivity (hyposensitivity) where the child doesn't register temperature changes adequately and may not notice they're too hot or too cold until dysregulation occurs; or sensory seeking where the child craves extreme temperature experiences. Temperature processing involves both exteroception (sensing temperature externally through skin) and interoception (sensing internal body temperature and comfort). Children with temperature processing differences may have difficulty with weather-appropriate clothing, food/drink temperatures, water temperature, touching surfaces of different temperatures, or transitioning between temperature environments. These differences can significantly impact daily functioning, safety, and participation in activities.

mainEntity.name: What is Temperature Sensory Processing Differences / Thermal Sensory Modulation Disorder / Interoceptive Processing Differences (Temperature) / Thermoregulatory Dysfunction / Sensory Over-Responsivity to Temperature / Sensory Under-Responsivity to Temperature?

acceptedAnswer.text: Temperature processing differences refer to atypical responses to thermal sensory input. These differences can manifest as: over-responsivity (hypersensitivity) where normal temperature variations feel extreme and uncomfortable, triggering avoidance or distress; under-responsivity (hyposensitivity) where the child doesn't register temperature changes adequately and may not notice they're too hot or too cold until dysregulation occurs; or sensory seeking where the child craves extreme temperature experiences. Temperature processing involves both exteroception (sensing temperature externally through skin) and interoception (sensing internal body temperature and comfort). Children with temperature processing differences may have difficulty with weather-appropriate clothing, food/drink temperatures, water temperature, touching surfaces of different temperatures, or transitioning between temperature environments. These differences can significantly impact daily functioning, safety, and participation in activities. Common signs include: Refuses weather-appropriate clothing (no coat in cold, won't remove layers in heat), Extreme reactions to touching cold or hot surfaces, Very narrow acceptable range for bath/shower water temperature, Strong preferences for food/drink temperature (only ice cold drinks, only hot food), Doesn't seem to notice getting too hot or too cold until dysregulated.

mainEntity.name: How does a Temperature Sensory Bin Kit help with temperature sensory processing differences?

acceptedAnswer.text: A temperature sensory bin allows controlled, playful exposure to different temperatures in a safe, low-pressure context. Using containers of warm water, cool water, ice, and room temperature materials, children can explore temperature through play rather than confrontation. This gradual exposure helps desensitize over-responsive children while building awareness in under-responsive children. The play context reduces anxiety around temperature and allows the child to control their exposure level. Comparing temperatures side by side builds the ability to discriminate between warm, cool, cold, and hot. Over time, this expands the child's tolerance and awareness. Price: ₹500–1,500. DIY option: Create temperature exploration bins using: containers of ice water, cool water, room temperature water, and warm water. Add scoops, cups, and small toys for play. Include materials that hold temperature: frozen toys, warmed rice bags, room temperature sand. Start with temperatures close to the child's comfortable range and gradually introduce more variation. Let the child lead exploration at their pace. Narrate temperature as they explore: 'That ice is very cold. This water is warm.' Compare and contrast: 'Which is cooler?' Never force immersion in uncomfortable temperatures — let the child touch briefly and build from there. Regular short sessions build tolerance over time. Safety: Always test water temperature before use. Never use water hot enough to burn. Supervise closely. Ensure child cannot drink dirty water. Watch for signs of distress and respect child's limits.

mainEntity.name: How does a Weighted Lap Pad with Temperature Insert help with temperature sensory processing differences?

acceptedAnswer.text: A weighted lap pad provides calming proprioceptive input that helps regulate the sensory system overall, making temperature input more manageable. Some weighted pads include pockets for heating or cooling inserts, allowing the child to combine the calming weight with gentle temperature exposure. The weight grounds and organizes the nervous system, reducing the 'alarm' response that over-responsive children may have to temperature. The gradual, controlled temperature exposure through a familiar, comforting item builds tolerance. For under-responsive children, the combined input (weight + temperature) provides more noticeable sensory information that helps build awareness. Price: ₹1,500–4,000. DIY option: Select a weighted lap pad appropriate for child's size (typically 1-2 kg for children). Choose one with pockets for inserts, or add a pocket yourself. Create temperature inserts: rice bag for warming in microwave, gel pack that can be refrigerated or frozen. Start with temperatures barely different from room temperature and gradually introduce more variation. Use the pad during calming activities (reading, screen time, quiet play) to associate temperature with regulation. Track which temperatures are calming vs. alerting for your child. For over-responsive children, start with very mild temperature and build tolerance. For under-responsive children, slightly more pronounced temperature may help build awareness. Safety: Check temperature of heated inserts before placing on child. Never overheat in microwave. Ensure weight is appropriate for child's size. Supervise use with temperature inserts.

mainEntity.name: How does a Interoception Activity Cards help with temperature sensory processing differences?

acceptedAnswer.text: Interoception is the sense of what's happening inside the body, including temperature awareness. Many children with temperature processing differences have difficulty recognizing their internal temperature state until they're already uncomfortable or dysregulated. Interoception activity cards guide structured activities that build body awareness, including noticing 'Am I hot?' 'Am I cold?' 'Am I comfortable?' This explicit teaching helps children develop the internal awareness they need to recognize temperature needs before crisis point. Cards provide visual structure for practicing body check-ins and building the temperature vocabulary needed to communicate comfort levels. Price: ₹300–1,200. DIY option: Create interoception cards focusing on temperature: 'Check your body. Are you hot, cold, or just right?' 'Notice your skin. Is it warm? Cool? Sweaty? Dry?' 'Notice your cheeks. Are they warm or cool?' Include activities: 'Go to a warm room. Notice how your body feels. Now go to a cooler room. What changed?' 'Hold something cold for 10 seconds. Notice your hand. Now hold something warm. What's different?' Practice body check-ins throughout the day: before going outside, after activity, during transitions. Build vocabulary: 'My hands feel cold but my body feels okay.' 'I'm starting to feel too warm.' Connect body awareness to action: 'You noticed you're warm. What could you do?' Safety: No specific safety concerns. Use developmentally appropriate language and activities.

mainEntity.name: How does a Layering Clothing System with Visuals help with temperature sensory processing differences?

acceptedAnswer.text: For children with temperature processing differences, a visual layering system provides concrete guidance for clothing decisions that their internal sensing may not reliably provide. Visual charts showing 'What to wear when' with temperature ranges and corresponding clothing layers remove the daily battle and build the decision-making scaffold the child needs. The system teaches logical temperature-clothing connections explicitly rather than relying on internal sensing. Over time, the child internalizes these connections. Layering specifically allows for adjustment without complete outfit changes, providing flexibility for children whose temperature perception fluctuates. Price: ₹200–800. DIY option: Create a visual temperature-clothing chart: show thermometer or temperature ranges with corresponding clothing pictures. Include layers that can be added/removed: base layer, mid layer, outer layer. Organize child's wardrobe by layers: base layers in one section, mid layers in another, outer layers accessible. Create a morning routine visual: 'Check temperature (thermometer, app, or looking outside) → Find on chart → Choose layers.' Include 'adjustment' strategies: 'If you feel hot, you can remove a layer. If you feel cold, add a layer.' Practice using the system daily. Review at end of day: 'Did your layers work today?' Adjust chart based on child's actual needs (their 'comfortable' may differ from typical). Safety: Ensure clothing choices are still weather-safe even if child's preferences differ. Supervise to prevent overheating or hypothermia risk.

mainEntity.name: How does a Thermometer Set (Ambient & Body) help with temperature sensory processing differences?

acceptedAnswer.text: Thermometers provide objective temperature information that children with processing differences may not accurately perceive internally. A room thermometer shows 'It's 22 degrees — that's comfortable' even if the child feels too hot or too cold. A body thermometer confirms 'Your body is 37 degrees — normal' even if they feel wrong. This objective data helps recalibrate perception over time and provides a shared reference point for discussions about clothing, activity, and comfort. For children who don't notice temperature until dysregulation, scheduled thermometer check-ins build awareness habits. Visual thermometer displays are especially helpful for children who learn visually. Price: ₹300–1,500. DIY option: Place visible room thermometers in key locations (bedroom, main living area, near door for checking before going outside). Use digital thermometers with clear displays. Create a reference chart: temperatures and what they mean ('15-20 = cool, you might want layers'). Practice daily check-ins: 'What does the thermometer say? How does your body feel? Do they match?' For under-responsive children, use scheduled temperature checks to build habit of noticing. For over-responsive children, use objective data to gently reality-check: 'The thermometer says 22 degrees — that's room temperature. Your body might feel hotter, but the room isn't actually hot.' Connect thermometer readings to clothing and comfort strategies. Safety: Body thermometers should be used safely and hygienically. Teach appropriate thermometer use. Do not use glass thermometers with young children.

mainEntity.name: How does a Cooling Towels and Wraps help with temperature sensory processing differences?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cooling towels and wraps provide controlled, portable cooling that can prevent or address overheating for temperature-sensitive children. For over-responsive children who feel too hot easily, cooling wraps provide relief that makes activities possible. For under-responsive children who don't notice overheating until crisis, proactive cooling during hot activities prevents dysregulation. Cooling towels work through evaporation and can be used around the neck, on the forehead, or draped on shoulders. Having a reliable cooling strategy available reduces anxiety about heat and expands what activities are possible. Price: ₹200–800. DIY option: Select cooling towels designed for evaporative cooling (wet, wring, wave to activate). Teach the child how to use: wet the towel, wring out excess, snap or wave to activate cooling. Practice in low-stakes situations before using in challenging environments. Create a 'cooling kit' for outings: cooling towels, water for re-wetting, portable fan. Use proactively: 'We're going to be outside in the heat — let's put on your cooling towel before we go.' For under-responsive children, schedule cooling breaks during hot activities even if child doesn't ask. Include neck wraps, headbands, or wristbands depending on child's preference. Some children prefer cold water bottles held against skin — include whatever works. Safety: Monitor for signs of heat illness even when using cooling strategies. Cooling towels are aids, not substitutes for appropriate heat safety. Ensure child stays hydrated.

@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Temperature Sensory Processing Differences
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Temperature processing is a complex sensory function involving both exteroception (external temperature sensing via skin) and interoception (internal body temperature sensing). Differences in temperature processing are often part of broader sensory processing patterns and commonly co-occur with autism, ADHD, and sensory processing disorder. Intervention focuses on building awareness (especially interoceptive awareness of body temperature state), expanding tolerance through gradual, controlled exposure, and developing strategies for managing temperature challenges in daily life. The goal is not to make the child 'normal' but to give them tools to recognize their temperature experience, communicate about it, and participate in activities despite temperature sensitivities.

supply.name: Weighted Lap Pad with Temperature Insert

step.name: Understand the Problem
step.text: Temperature processing differences refer to atypical responses to thermal sensory input. These differences can manifest as: over-responsivity (hypersensitivity) where normal temperature variations feel extreme and uncomfortable, triggering avoidance or distress; under-responsivity (hyposensitivity) where the child doesn't register temperature changes adequately and may not notice they're too hot or too cold until dysregulation occurs; or sensory seeking where the child craves extreme temperature experiences. Temperature processing involves both exteroception (sensing temperature externally through skin) and interoception (sensing internal body temperature and comfort). Children with temperature processing differences may have difficulty with weather-appropriate clothing, food/drink temperatures, water temperature, touching surfaces of different temperatures, or transitioning between temperature environments. These differences can significantly impact daily functioning, safety, and participation in activities. Temperature processing is a complex sensory function involving both exteroception (external temperature sensing via skin) and interoception (internal body temperature sensing). Differences in temperature processing are often part of broader sensory processing patterns and commonly co-occur with autism, ADHD, and sensory processing disorder. Intervention focuses on building awareness (especially interoceptive awareness of body temperature state), expanding tolerance through gradual, controlled exposure, and developing strategies for managing temperature challenges in daily life. The goal is not to make the child 'normal' but to give them tools to recognize their temperature experience, communicate about it, and participate in activities despite temperature sensitivities.

step.text: Begin with: Temperature Sensory Bin Kit, Weighted Lap Pad with Temperature Insert, Interoception Activity Cards.

about.description: Temperature processing differences refer to atypical responses to thermal sensory input. These differences can manifest as: over-responsivity (hypersensitivity) where normal temperature variations feel extreme and uncomfortable, triggering avoidance or distress; under-responsivity (hyposensitivity) where the child doesn't register temperature changes adequately and may not notice they're too hot or too cold until dysregulation occurs; or sensory seeking where the child craves extreme temperature experiences. Temperature processing involves both exteroception (sensing temperature externally through skin) and interoception (sensing internal body temperature and comfort). signOrSymptom.name: Refuses weather-appropriate clothing (no coat in cold, won't remove layers in heat)
Extreme reactions to touching cold or hot surfaces
Very narrow acceptable range for bath/shower water temperature
Strong preferences for food/drink temperature (only ice cold drinks, only hot food)
Doesn't seem to notice getting too hot or too cold until dysregulated
Reports being hot when others are cold, or vice versa
Meltdowns in temperature transitions (going outside in different weather)
Seeks out extreme temperatures (very hot baths, ice on skin)

item.name: Interoception Activity Cards
item.description: Interoception is the sense of what's happening inside the body, including temperature awareness. Many children with temperature processing differences have difficulty recognizing their internal temperature state until they're already uncomfortable or dysregulated. Interoception activity cards guide structured activities that build body awareness, including noticing 'Am I hot?' 'Am I cold?' 'Am I comfortable?' This explicit teaching helps children develop the internal awareness they need to recognize temperature needs before crisis point. Cards provide visual structure for practicing body check-ins and building the temperature vocabulary needed to communicate comfort levels.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create interoception cards focusing on temperature: 'Check your body. Are you hot, cold, or just right?' 'Notice your skin. Is it warm? Cool? Sweaty? Dry?' 'Notice your cheeks. Are they warm or cool?' Include activities: 'Go to a warm room. Notice how your body feels. Now go to a cooler room. What changed?' 'Hold something cold for 10 seconds. Notice your hand. Now hold something warm. What's different?' Practice body check-ins throughout the day: before going outside, after activity, during transitions. Build vocabulary: 'My hands feel cold but my body feels okay.' 'I'm starting to feel too warm.' Connect body awareness to action: 'You noticed you're warm. What could you do?'

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: No specific safety concerns. Use developmentally appropriate language and activities.

item.name: Layering Clothing System with Visuals
item.description: For children with temperature processing differences, a visual layering system provides concrete guidance for clothing decisions that their internal sensing may not reliably provide. Visual charts showing 'What to wear when' with temperature ranges and corresponding clothing layers remove the daily battle and build the decision-making scaffold the child needs. The system teaches logical temperature-clothing connections explicitly rather than relying on internal sensing. Over time, the child internalizes these connections. Layering specifically allows for adjustment without complete outfit changes, providing flexibility for children whose temperature perception fluctuates.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a visual temperature-clothing chart: show thermometer or temperature ranges with corresponding clothing pictures. Include layers that can be added/removed: base layer, mid layer, outer layer. Organize child's wardrobe by layers: base layers in one section, mid layers in another, outer layers accessible. Create a morning routine visual: 'Check temperature (thermometer, app, or looking outside) → Find on chart → Choose layers.' Include 'adjustment' strategies: 'If you feel hot, you can remove a layer. If you feel cold, add a layer.' Practice using the system daily. Review at end of day: 'Did your layers work today?' Adjust chart based on child's actual needs (their 'comfortable' may differ from typical).

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure clothing choices are still weather-safe even if child's preferences differ. Supervise to prevent overheating or hypothermia risk.

item.name: Thermometer Set (Ambient & Body)
item.description: Thermometers provide objective temperature information that children with processing differences may not accurately perceive internally. A room thermometer shows 'It's 22 degrees — that's comfortable' even if the child feels too hot or too cold. A body thermometer confirms 'Your body is 37 degrees — normal' even if they feel wrong. This objective data helps recalibrate perception over time and provides a shared reference point for discussions about clothing, activity, and comfort. For children who don't notice temperature until dysregulation, scheduled thermometer check-ins build awareness habits. Visual thermometer displays are especially helpful for children who learn visually.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Place visible room thermometers in key locations (bedroom, main living area, near door for checking before going outside). Use digital thermometers with clear displays. Create a reference chart: temperatures and what they mean ('15-20 = cool, you might want layers'). Practice daily check-ins: 'What does the thermometer say? How does your body feel? Do they match?' For under-responsive children, use scheduled temperature checks to build habit of noticing. For over-responsive children, use objective data to gently reality-check: 'The thermometer says 22 degrees — that's room temperature. Your body might feel hotter, but the room isn't actually hot.' Connect thermometer readings to clothing and comfort strategies.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Body thermometers should be used safely and hygienically. Teach appropriate thermometer use. Do not use glass thermometers with young children.

item.name: Cooling Towels and Wraps
item.description: Cooling towels and wraps provide controlled, portable cooling that can prevent or address overheating for temperature-sensitive children. For over-responsive children who feel too hot easily, cooling wraps provide relief that makes activities possible. For under-responsive children who don't notice overheating until crisis, proactive cooling during hot activities prevents dysregulation. Cooling towels work through evaporation and can be used around the neck, on the forehead, or draped on shoulders. Having a reliable cooling strategy available reduces anxiety about heat and expands what activities are possible.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Select cooling towels designed for evaporative cooling (wet, wring, wave to activate). Teach the child how to use: wet the towel, wring out excess, snap or wave to activate cooling. Practice in low-stakes situations before using in challenging environments. Create a 'cooling kit' for outings: cooling towels, water for re-wetting, portable fan. Use proactively: 'We're going to be outside in the heat — let's put on your cooling towel before we go.' For under-responsive children, schedule cooling breaks during hot activities even if child doesn't ask. Include neck wraps, headbands, or wristbands depending on child's preference. Some children prefer cold water bottles held against skin — include whatever works.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Monitor for signs of heat illness even when using cooling strategies. Cooling towels are aids, not substitutes for appropriate heat safety. Ensure child stays hydrated.

item.name: Warming and Cooling Fidgets
item.description: Temperature-responsive fidgets provide safe, controlled exposure to temperature through a familiar, comforting format. Some fidgets can be frozen (gel-filled fidgets, certain putties) or warmed (hand warmers, microwavable fidgets). Using temperature in a fidget context makes exposure playful rather than threatening. The child controls the interaction — they can touch briefly, hold longer, put down when uncomfortable. This builds tolerance gradually. For sensory seekers, temperature fidgets provide the input they crave safely. For over-responsive children, gentle exposure through a preferred item reduces threat response. Fidgets are also portable for temperature management on the go.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Gather or purchase fidgets that can hold temperature: gel bead stress balls (can be frozen), therapy putty (can be warmed), fabric fidgets (can be heated or cooled), gel packs in soft covers, reusable hand warmers. Test temperatures before giving to child — never too hot to handle safely. Introduce temperature fidgets during calm activities. Let child explore: 'This one just came from the freezer. Feel how cold! This one is room temperature.' Progress to intentional use: 'Your hands feel cold — want to try the warm fidget?' Keep temperature fidgets accessible for self-regulation. Pack in sensory kit for outings. Track which temperatures are calming vs. alerting for your child.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Test temperature before handing to child. Never overheat in microwave. Supervise use with younger children. Check fidgets for damage before use.

item.name: Thermal Blankets (Heated/Cooling Options)
item.description: Thermal blankets provide whole-body temperature regulation in a comforting, enveloping format. For children who feel cold when others don't, a heated blanket provides the warmth they need to function. For children who overheat easily, cooling blankets offer relief. The blanket format provides proprioceptive input (pressure, wrapping) alongside temperature, enhancing the calming effect. Thermal blankets can be used for designated 'regulation breaks' — a safe, predictable temperature environment the child can access when overwhelmed. Having reliable temperature regulation tools reduces anxiety about temperature exposure in general.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Select thermal blankets based on child's needs: heated blankets for cold-sensitive children (electric or microwavable), cooling blankets for heat-sensitive children (gel-infused or breathable technology). Create a designated 'comfort zone' where the thermal blanket lives — child can access for temperature regulation breaks. Establish use routines: 'If you're feeling too cold, you can get your warm blanket.' For under-responsive children, offer blanket proactively when environment suggests need. Practice using as regulation tool during calm times before relying on it during distress. Some children prefer weighted blankets with temperature properties — combination of pressure and temperature is powerful.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Electric blankets require supervision. Never use with child who might fall asleep unattended. Check temperature settings before use. Ensure blanket is in good condition with no damaged wiring. Follow manufacturer guidelines.

item.name: Temperature Gradation Toys
item.description: Temperature gradation toys are sets of objects at different temperatures (or designed to feel like different temperatures) that teach temperature discrimination and vocabulary. By comparing objects and ordering them from cold to warm, children build the fine discrimination needed to understand their own temperature experience. This explicit teaching develops awareness that under-responsive children may lack and provides over-responsive children practice with temperature in a controlled, non-threatening context. Gradation activities can include ice, refrigerated items, room temperature items, and warmed items — progressing through the spectrum. The activity format (sorting, ordering, matching) makes temperature exploration structured and purposeful.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a temperature gradation activity: gather objects that can be at different temperatures — metal spoons (freeze some, refrigerate some, room temp, warm some in warm water), fabric pieces (frozen, refrigerated, room temp, warmed), containers of water at different temperatures. Present as sorting activity: 'Put these in order from coldest to warmest.' Start with obvious differences (ice vs. warm) and progress to subtle differences. Build vocabulary: 'freezing cold, cold, cool, room temperature, warm, hot.' Connect to body awareness: 'Which one feels most comfortable to touch?' Use activity to practice tolerance: 'Touch the cold one for 5 seconds, then the warm one.' Progress to matching games: 'Find another object that's the same temperature.'

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Test temperatures before child touches. Avoid extremes. Watch for any distress and respect child's limits.

socialMedia.caption: When hot feels unbearable or cold doesn't register. Some children experience temperature differently — too sensitive, not sensitive enough, or seeking extremes. Here are 9 materials that help: temperature sensory bins, weighted pads with inserts, interoception cards, layering systems, thermometers, cooling towels, temperature fidgets, thermal blankets, and gradation toys. Build awareness and tolerance. 🔖 #TemperatureSensitivity #SensoryProcessing #PinnacleBlooms
